items.name: ChainIterator
items.signature: public struct ChainIterator[A, B] where A: Iterator, B: Iterator, B.Item == A.Item { /* private fields */ }
items.doc:
Chains two iterators together.
First yields all elements from first, then all from second.

members.name: init
members.doc: Creates an iterator that chains first and second together.

members.name: next
members.doc: Returns the next element from first, or from second if first is exhausted.

items.name: CycleIterator
items.signature: public struct CycleIterator[I] where I: Iterator { /* private fields */ }
items.doc: Repeats an iterator forever by cloning it when exhausted.

members.name: init
members.doc: Creates an iterator that repeats iter infinitely.

members.name: next
members.doc: Returns the next element, restarting from the beginning if needed.

items.name: DoubleEndedIterator
items.signature: public protocol DoubleEndedIterator

members.name: nextBack
members.doc:
Returns the next element from the back, or None if exhausted.

Repeated calls will return elements in reverse order until the
iterator is exhausted. Can be interleaved with `next()` calls.

members.name: rev
members.doc:
Returns an iterator that yields elements in reverse order.

Unlike collecting and reversing, this is lazy and O(1) to create.

Example:
    [1, 2, 3, 4, 5].iter().rev().collect()  // [5, 4, 3, 2, 1]

    // Take last 3 elements efficiently
    [1, 2, 3, 4, 5].iter().rev().take(count: 3).collect()  // [5, 4, 3]

    // Find last matching element
    [1, 2, 3, 4, 5].iter().rev().find({ it % 2 == 0 })  // Some(4)

members.name: Item
members.doc: The type of elements yielded by this iterator.

members.name: next
members.doc:
Returns the next element, or None if the sequence is exhausted.

Once None is returned, subsequent calls should continue to return None
(though `fuse()` can enforce this guarantee).

items.name: EmptyIterator
items.signature: public struct EmptyIterator[T] { /* private fields */ }
items.doc: An iterator that yields nothing.

members.name: init
members.doc: Creates an empty iterator.

members.name: next
members.doc: Always returns None.

items.name: EnumerateIterator
items.signature: public struct EnumerateIterator[I] where I: Iterator { /* private fields */ }
items.doc: Yields (index, item) pairs.

members.name: init
members.doc: Creates an iterator that pairs each element with its zero-based index.

members.name: next
members.doc: Returns the next (index, element) pair, or None if exhausted.

items.name: ExactSizeIterator
items.signature: public protocol ExactSizeIterator

members.name: isEmpty
members.doc:
Returns true if the iterator has no more elements.

Equivalent to `remaining == 0`.

members.name: remaining
members.doc:
Returns the exact number of remaining elements.

This value decreases by 1 for each `next()` call that returns Some.
When this returns 0, `next()` will return None.

members.name: Item
members.doc: The type of elements yielded by this iterator.

members.name: next
members.doc:
Returns the next element, or None if the sequence is exhausted.

Once None is returned, subsequent calls should continue to return None
(though `fuse()` can enforce this guarantee).

items.name: FilterIterator
items.signature: public struct FilterIterator[I] where I: Iterator { /* private fields */ }
items.doc: Yields only elements matching a predicate.

members.name: init
members.doc: Creates a filter iterator that yields only elements where predicate returns true.

members.name: next
members.doc: Returns the next matching element, or None if exhausted.

items.name: FilterMapIterator
items.signature: public struct FilterMapIterator[I, U] where I: Iterator { /* private fields */ }
items.doc:
Filters and transforms in one step.
Elements where transform returns None are skipped.

members.name: init
members.doc: Creates an iterator that applies transform and yields only Some results.

members.name: next
members.doc: Returns the next transformed element, or None if exhausted.

items.name: FlatMapIterator
items.signature: public struct FlatMapIterator[I, U] where I: Iterator, U: Iterator { /* private fields */ }
items.doc:
Transforms each element into an iterator and flattens the results.

Each element is transformed into an iterator, and all resulting
elements are yielded sequentially.

members.name: init
members.doc: Creates an iterator that applies transform to each element and flattens.

members.name: next
members.doc: Returns the next element from the flattened sequence, or None if exhausted.

items.name: FlattenIterator
items.signature: public struct FlattenIterator[I] where I: Iterator, I.Item: Iterator { /* private fields */ }
items.doc:
Flattens nested iterators into a single iterator.

Takes an iterator of iterators and yields all elements from each
inner iterator sequentially.

members.name: init
members.doc: Creates an iterator that flattens nested iterators.

members.name: next
members.doc: Returns the next element from the flattened sequence.

items.name: FuseIterator
items.signature: public struct FuseIterator[I] where I: Iterator { /* private fields */ }
items.doc:
Stops permanently after yielding None once.
Useful for iterators that might resume after None.

members.name: init
members.doc: Creates a fused iterator that stops permanently after the first None.

members.name: next
members.doc: Returns the next element, or None permanently after first exhaustion.

items.name: InspectIterator
items.signature: public struct InspectIterator[I] where I: Iterator { /* private fields */ }
items.doc:
Calls a function on each element as it passes through.

Useful for debugging or logging without affecting the iterator chain.
The inspector function receives a reference to each element.

members.name: init
members.doc: Creates an iterator that calls inspector on each element.

members.name: next
members.doc: Returns the next element after calling the inspector on it.

items.name: IntersperseIterator
items.signature: public struct IntersperseIterator[I] where I: Iterator { /* private fields */ }
items.doc: Inserts a separator between each element.

members.name: init
members.doc: Creates an iterator that inserts separator between elements.

members.name: next
members.doc: Returns the next element or separator.

items.name: IntersperseWithIterator
items.signature: public struct IntersperseWithIterator[I] where I: Iterator { /* private fields */ }
items.doc: Inserts a lazily-generated separator between each element.

members.name: init
members.doc: Creates an iterator that inserts separators generated by the function.

members.name: next
members.doc: Returns the next element or generated separator.

items.name: Iterable
items.signature: public protocol Iterable

members.name: Item
members.doc: The type of elements produced by iteration.

members.name: Iter
members.doc: The type of iterator that will be produced.

members.name: iter
members.doc: Creates an iterator over this collection's elements.

items.name: Iterator
items.signature: public protocol Iterator

members.name: all
members.signature: public mutating func all((Item) -> Bool) -> Bool
members.doc:
Returns true if all elements satisfy the predicate.

Short-circuits on first non-match.
Returns true for an empty iterator (vacuous truth).

Example:
    [2, 4, 6].iter().all({ it % 2 == 0 })  // true
    [2, 3, 4].iter().all({ it % 2 == 0 })  // false (stops at 3)
    [].iter().all({ false })               // true (empty)

members.name: any
members.signature: public mutating func any((Item) -> Bool) -> Bool
members.doc:
Returns true if any element satisfies the predicate.

Short-circuits on first match (doesn't consume remaining elements).
Returns false for an empty iterator.

Example:
    [1, 2, 3, 4].iter().any({ it > 3 })  // true (stops at 4)
    [1, 2, 3].iter().any({ it > 10 })    // false
    [].iter().any({ true })              // false

members.name: chain
members.signature: public func chain[Other](Other) -> ChainIterator[Self, Other] where Other: Iterator, Other.Item == Item
members.doc:
Chains another iterator after this one.

Yields all elements from self, then all from other.

Example:
    [1, 2].iter().chain(other: [3, 4].iter()).collect()  // [1, 2, 3, 4]

    // Concatenate multiple iterators
    a.iter().chain(other: b.iter()).chain(other: c.iter())

members.name: collect
members.doc:
Collects all elements into an array.

Consumes the entire iterator.

Example:
    [1, 2, 3].iter().filter({ it > 1 }).collect()  // [2, 3]
    (1..5).iter().map({ it * it }).collect()  // [1, 4, 9, 16]

members.name: compactMap
members.signature: public func compactMap[T]() -> FilterMapIterator[Self, T] where Item == Optional[T]
members.doc:
Filters out None values and unwraps the Some values.

Equivalent to `filterMap({ it })` but more readable when the elements
are already optionals.

Example:
    let maybeNumbers: [Int64?] = [Some(1), None, Some(2), None, Some(3)]
    maybeNumbers.iter().compactMap().collect()  // [1, 2, 3]

    // With transformation
    ["1", "two", "3"].iter()
        .map({ Int64.parse(it) })  // [Some(1), None, Some(3)]
        .compactMap()              // [1, 3]

members.name: contains
members.doc:
Returns true if any element equals the given value.

Short-circuits on first match.

Example:
    [1, 2, 3].iter().contains(element: 2)  // true
    [1, 2, 3].iter().contains(element: 5)  // false

members.name: count
members.doc:
Returns the number of elements.

Consumes the entire iterator.

Example:
    [1, 2, 3, 4, 5].iter().filter({ it % 2 == 0 }).count()  // 2

members.name: cycle
members.doc:
Repeats this iterator forever by copying when exhausted.

WARNING: Creates an infinite iterator. Use with `take()` to limit.

Example:
    [1, 2, 3].iter().cycle().take(count: 7).collect()
    // [1, 2, 3, 1, 2, 3, 1]

members.name: enumerate
filter
members.doc:
Yields only elements satisfying the predicate.

Lazy - elements are tested as they are consumed.

Example:
    [1, 2, 3, 4, 5].iter().filter({ it % 2 == 0 }).collect()  // [2, 4]
    ["", "a", "", "b"].iter().filter({ !it.isEmpty }).collect()  // ["a", "b"]

members.name: filterMap
members.signature: public func filterMap[U]((Item) -> U?) -> FilterMapIterator[Self, U]
members.doc:
Transforms elements and filters out None results in one step.

Combines map and filter - useful when transformation might fail.

Example:
    ["1", "two", "3"].iter()
        .filterMap({ Int64.parse(it) })
        .collect()  // [1, 3]

members.name: find
members.signature: public mutating func find((Item) -> Bool) -> Item?
members.doc:
Returns the first element satisfying the predicate, or None.

Short-circuits on first match.

Example:
    [1, 2, 3, 4, 5].iter().find({ it > 3 })   // Some(4)
    [1, 2, 3].iter().find({ it > 10 })        // None

members.name: first
members.doc:
Returns the first element, or None if empty.

Consumes only the first element.

Example:
    [1, 2, 3].iter().first()  // Some(1)
    [].iter().first()         // None

members.name: flatMap
members.signature: public func flatMap[U]((Item) -> U) -> FlatMapIterator[Self, U] where U: Iterator
members.doc:
Transforms each element into an iterator and flattens the results.

Each element is passed to transform, which returns an iterator.
All elements from all resulting iterators are yielded sequentially.

Example:
    [[1, 2], [3, 4], [5]].iter()
        .flatMap({ it.iter() })
        .collect()  // [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]

    // Get all characters from all words
    ["hello", "world"].iter()
        .flatMap({ it.chars() })
        .collect()  // ['h', 'e', 'l', 'l', 'o', 'w', 'o', 'r', 'l', 'd']

    // Filter and expand in one step
    [1, 2, 3].iter()
        .flatMap({ if it % 2 == 0 { [it, it].iter() } else { [].iter() } })
        .collect()  // [2, 2]

members.name: flatten
members.doc:
Flattens nested iterators into a single iterator.

Each inner iterator is fully consumed before moving to the next.

Example:
    let nested = [[1, 2], [3, 4], [5]].iter().map({ it.iter() })
    nested.flatten().collect()  // [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]

    // Equivalent to flatMap when you already have iterators
    let iters: [ArrayIterator[Int64]] = ...
    iters.iter().flatten().collect()

members.name: fold
members.signature: public consuming func fold[Acc](initial: Acc, combine: (Acc, Item) -> Acc) -> Acc
members.doc:
Reduces elements to a single value using an accumulator.

Starts with initial value and applies combine(accumulator, element)
for each element. Returns initial if the iterator is empty.

Example:
    [1, 2, 3, 4].iter().fold(initial: 0, combine: |acc, x| acc + x)  // 10
    [1, 2, 3].iter().fold(initial: 1, combine: |acc, x| acc * x)  // 6
    [].iter().fold(initial: 42, combine: |acc, x| acc + x)  // 42

members.name: forEach
members.doc:
Calls action on each element.

Consumes the entire iterator. Use when you need side effects.

Example:
    [1, 2, 3].iter().forEach({ print(it) })

members.name: fuse
inspect
intersperse
intersperseWith
members.doc:
Inserts separators generated by a function between each pair of elements.

Useful when the separator is expensive to create or needs to vary.

Example:
    var counter = 0
    [1, 2, 3].iter()
        .intersperseWith(separator: || { counter += 1; counter * 10 })
        .collect()
    // [1, 10, 2, 20, 3]

members.name: isSorted
members.doc:
Returns true if the iterator yields elements in sorted (ascending) order.

Consumes the entire iterator. Returns true for empty or single-element
iterators.

Example:
    [1, 2, 3, 4, 5].iter().isSorted()  // true
    [1, 3, 2, 4, 5].iter().isSorted()  // false
    [1, 1, 2, 2, 3].iter().isSorted()  // true (equal elements OK)
    [].iter().isSorted()               // true
    [42].iter().isSorted()             // true

    // Short-circuits on first out-of-order pair
    [1, 0, 2, 3, 4, 5, ...].iter().isSorted()  // false (stops at 1, 0)

members.name: isSorted
members.signature: public consuming func isSorted(by: (Item, Item) -> Bool) -> Bool
members.doc:
Returns true if elements are sorted according to the comparator.

The comparator should return true if the first argument should come
before the second (i.e., they are in the correct order).

Example:
    // Check descending order
    [5, 4, 3, 2, 1].iter().isSorted(by: |a, b| a >= b)  // true

    // Check sorted by absolute value
    [-1, 2, -3, 4].iter().isSorted(by: |a, b| a.abs() <= b.abs())  // true

    // Case-insensitive string sorting
    ["Apple", "banana", "Cherry"].iter()
        .isSorted(by: |a, b| a.lowercase() <= b.lowercase())  // true

members.name: isSortedBy
members.signature: public consuming func isSortedBy[K]((Item) -> K) -> Bool where K: Comparable
members.doc:
Returns true if elements are sorted by the given key in ascending order.

Example:
    let people = [("Alice", 25), ("Bob", 30), ("Charlie", 35)]
    people.iter().isSortedBy(key: { it.1 })  // true (sorted by age)

    let words = ["a", "bb", "ccc"]
    words.iter().isSortedBy(key: { it.count })  // true (sorted by length)

members.name: isSortedDescending
members.doc:
Returns true if elements are sorted in descending order.

Example:
    [5, 4, 3, 2, 1].iter().isSortedDescending()  // true
    [5, 3, 4, 2, 1].iter().isSortedDescending()  // false

members.name: last
members.doc:
Returns the last element, or None if empty.

Consumes the entire iterator.

Example:
    [1, 2, 3].iter().last()  // Some(3)
    [].iter().last()         // None

members.name: map
members.signature: public func map[U]((Item) -> U) -> MapIterator[Self, U]
members.doc:
Transforms each element using the given function.

Lazy - transformation happens as elements are consumed.

Example:
    [1, 2, 3].iter().map({ it * 2 }).collect()  // [2, 4, 6]
    ["hello", "world"].iter().map({ it.count }).collect()  // [5, 5]

members.name: max
members.doc:
Returns the maximum element, or None if empty.

Consumes the entire iterator.

Example:
    [3, 1, 4, 1, 5].iter().max()  // Some(5)
    [].iter().max()               // None

members.name: maxBy
members.signature: public consuming func maxBy[K]((Item) -> K) -> Item? where K: Comparable
members.doc:
Returns the element with the maximum value of the key function.

Consumes the entire iterator. Returns None if empty.
If multiple elements have the maximum key, returns the first.

Example:
    let people = [("Alice", 30), ("Bob", 25), ("Charlie", 35)]
    people.iter().maxBy(key: { it.1 })  // Some(("Charlie", 35))

    let words = ["hello", "hi", "hey"]
    words.iter().maxBy(key: { it.count })  // Some("hello")

    [].iter().maxBy(key: { it })  // None

members.name: min
members.doc:
Returns the minimum element, or None if empty.

Consumes the entire iterator.

Example:
    [3, 1, 4, 1, 5].iter().min()  // Some(1)
    [].iter().min()               // None

members.name: minBy
members.signature: public consuming func minBy[K]((Item) -> K) -> Item? where K: Comparable
members.doc:
Returns the element with the minimum value of the key function.

Consumes the entire iterator. Returns None if empty.
If multiple elements have the minimum key, returns the first.

Example:
    let people = [("Alice", 30), ("Bob", 25), ("Charlie", 35)]
    people.iter().minBy(key: { it.1 })  // Some(("Bob", 25))

    let words = ["hello", "hi", "hey"]
    words.iter().minBy(key: { it.count })  // Some("hi")

    [].iter().minBy(key: { it })  // None

members.name: next
members.doc:
Returns the next element, or None if the sequence is exhausted.

Once None is returned, subsequent calls should continue to return None
(though `fuse()` can enforce this guarantee).

members.name: nth
members.doc:
Returns the nth element (zero-indexed), or None if out of bounds.

Consumes elements up to and including n.

Example:
    [10, 20, 30, 40].iter().nth(n: 2)  // Some(30)
    [10, 20].iter().nth(n: 5)          // None
    [10, 20, 30].iter().nth(n: 0)      // Some(10)

members.name: peekable
members.doc:
Wraps this iterator to allow peeking at the next element without consuming it.

Example:
    var iter = [1, 2, 3].iter().peekable()
    iter.peek()  // Some(1) - doesn't consume
    iter.peek()  // Some(1) - still 1
    iter.next()  // Some(1) - now consumed
    iter.peek()  // Some(2)

members.name: position
members.signature: public mutating func position((Item) -> Bool) -> Int64?
members.doc:
Returns the position of the first element satisfying the predicate, or None.

Short-circuits on first match.

Example:
    ["a", "b", "c"].iter().position({ it == "b" })  // Some(1)
    [1, 2, 3].iter().position({ it > 10 })          // None

members.name: product
members.doc:
Returns the product of all elements.

Consumes the entire iterator. Returns the multiplicative identity (one)
if the iterator is empty.

Example:
    [1, 2, 3, 4, 5].iter().product()  // 120
    [2.0, 3.0, 4.0].iter().product()  // 24.0
    [].iter().product()               // 1

    // Factorial via range
    (1..=5).iter().product()  // 120 (5!)

members.name: reduce
members.signature: public consuming func reduce(combine: (Item, Item) -> Item) -> Item?
members.doc:
Reduces elements using the first element as the initial accumulator.

Returns None if the iterator is empty.

Example:
    [1, 2, 3, 4].iter().reduce(combine: |a, b| a + b)  // Some(10)
    [5].iter().reduce(combine: |a, b| a + b)  // Some(5)
    [].iter().reduce(combine: |a, b| a + b)  // None

members.name: scan
members.signature: public func scan[Acc](Acc, (Acc, Item) -> Acc) -> ScanIterator[Self, Acc]
members.doc:
Yields running accumulator values, like fold but with intermediate results.

Starts with initial value, applies combine for each element, and yields
each intermediate accumulator value. Useful for running totals, prefixes, etc.

Example:
    // Running sum
    [1, 2, 3, 4].iter()
        .scan(initial: 0, combine: |acc, x| acc + x)
        .collect()  // [1, 3, 6, 10]

    // Running product
    [1, 2, 3, 4].iter()
        .scan(initial: 1, combine: |acc, x| acc * x)
        .collect()  // [1, 2, 6, 24]

    // Track state while iterating
    "aabbc".chars().iter()
        .scan(initial: 0, combine: |count, c| if c == 'b' { count + 1 } else { count })
        .collect()  // [0, 0, 1, 2, 2]

members.name: skip
members.doc:
Skips the first count elements.

Example:
    [1, 2, 3, 4, 5].iter().skip(count: 2).collect()  // [3, 4, 5]
    [1, 2].iter().skip(count: 10).collect()  // [] - all skipped

members.name: skipWhile
members.doc:
Skips elements while predicate returns true, then yields all remaining.

Once the predicate returns false, yields that element and all subsequent
elements without further testing.

Example:
    [1, 2, 3, 4, 1, 2].iter()
        .skipWhile({ it < 3 })
        .collect()  // [3, 4, 1, 2] - note 1, 2 at end included

members.name: sorted
members.doc:
Collects elements into a sorted array.

Consumes the entire iterator.

Example:
    [3, 1, 4, 1, 5].iter().sorted()  // [1, 1, 3, 4, 5]
    [3, 1, 2].iter().filter({ it > 1 }).sorted()  // [2, 3]

members.name: stepBy
sum
members.doc:
Returns the sum of all elements.

Consumes the entire iterator. Returns the additive identity (zero)
if the iterator is empty.

Example:
    [1, 2, 3, 4, 5].iter().sum()  // 15
    [1.5, 2.5, 3.0].iter().sum()  // 7.0
    [].iter().sum()               // 0

    // With filtering
    [1, 2, 3, 4, 5].iter().filter({ it % 2 == 0 }).sum()  // 6

members.name: take
members.doc:
Takes only the first count elements.

Stops iteration after yielding count elements, even if more are available.

Example:
    [1, 2, 3, 4, 5].iter().take(count: 3).collect()  // [1, 2, 3]
    [1, 2].iter().take(count: 10).collect()  // [1, 2] - fewer available

members.name: takeWhile
members.doc:
Takes elements while predicate returns true, then stops.

Stops at the first element that doesn't satisfy the predicate.
Does not include that element.

Example:
    [1, 2, 3, 4, 1, 2].iter()
        .takeWhile({ it < 4 })
        .collect()  // [1, 2, 3] - stops at 4

members.name: tryFold
members.signature: public mutating func tryFold[Acc, E](initial: Acc, combine: (Acc, Item) -> Result[Acc, E]) -> Result[Acc, E]

members.name: tryForEach
members.signature: public mutating func tryForEach[E]((Item) -> Result[(), E]) -> Result[(), E]
members.doc:
Calls action on each element with early exit on error.

Like `forEach`, but the action returns a Result. Stops on first Err.

Example:
    files.iter().tryForEach({ (path) in
        File.delete(path)  // Returns Result[(), IoError]
    })  // Stops on first deletion failure

members.name: unzip
members.signature: public consuming func unzip[A, B]() -> (Array[A], Array[B]) where Item == (A, B)

members.name: zip
members.signature: public func zip[Other](Other) -> ZipIterator[Self, Other] where Other: Iterator

members.name: Item
members.doc: The type of elements yielded by this iterator.

members.name: iter
members.doc: Returns self, allowing an iterator to be used where an iterable is expected.

items.name: MapIterator
items.signature: public struct MapIterator[I, U] where I: Iterator { /* private fields */ }
items.doc: Transforms each element using a function.

members.name: init
members.doc: Creates a map iterator that applies transform to each element of inner.

members.name: next
members.doc: Returns the next transformed element, or None if exhausted.

items.name: OnceIterator
items.signature: public struct OnceIterator[T] { /* private fields */ }
items.doc: An iterator that yields a single value.

members.name: init
members.doc: Creates an iterator that yields value exactly once.

members.name: next
members.doc: Returns the value on first call, None thereafter.

items.name: PeekableIterator
items.signature: public struct PeekableIterator[I] where I: Iterator { /* private fields */ }
items.doc: Allows peeking at the next element without consuming it.

members.name: init
members.doc: Creates a peekable iterator wrapping inner.

members.name: peek
members.doc: Returns the next element without consuming it.

members.name: next
members.doc: Returns and consumes the next element.

items.name: RepeatIterator
items.signature: public struct RepeatIterator[T] { /* private fields */ }
items.doc: An iterator that yields the same value forever.

members.name: init
members.doc: Creates an iterator that yields value forever.

members.name: next
members.doc: Returns a copy of the value.

items.name: RepeatNIterator
items.signature: public struct RepeatNIterator[T] { /* private fields */ }
items.doc: An iterator that yields the same value n times.

members.name: init
members.doc: Creates an iterator that yields value exactly count times.

members.name: next
members.doc: Returns a copy of the value, or None after count iterations.

items.name: RevIterator
items.signature: public struct RevIterator[I] where I: DoubleEndedIterator, I: Iterator { /* private fields */ }
items.doc:
Reverses a double-ended iterator.

Yields elements by calling `nextBack()` on the inner iterator.

members.name: init
members.doc: Creates an iterator that yields elements in reverse order.

members.name: next
members.doc: Returns the next element from the back of the inner iterator.

members.name: nextBack
members.doc: Returns the next element from the front of the inner iterator.

items.name: ScanIterator
items.signature: public struct ScanIterator[I, Acc] where I: Iterator { /* private fields */ }
items.doc:
Yields running accumulator values during a fold.

Like fold, but yields each intermediate accumulator value.

members.name: init
members.signature: public init(inner: I, initial: Acc, combine: (Acc, I.Item) -> Acc)
members.doc: Creates an iterator that yields running fold values.

members.name: next
members.doc: Returns the next accumulated value.

items.name: SkipIterator
items.signature: public struct SkipIterator[I] where I: Iterator { /* private fields */ }
items.doc: Skips the first n elements.

members.name: init
members.doc: Creates an iterator that skips the first count elements.

members.name: next
members.doc: Returns the next element after skipping, or None if exhausted.

items.name: SkipWhileIterator
items.signature: public struct SkipWhileIterator[I] where I: Iterator { /* private fields */ }
items.doc: Skips elements while predicate is true, then yields all remaining.

members.name: init
members.doc: Creates an iterator that skips elements until predicate returns false.

members.name: next
members.doc: Returns the next element after skipping is complete, or None.

items.name: StepByIterator
items.signature: public struct StepByIterator[I] where I: Iterator { /* private fields */ }
items.doc: Yields every nth element, starting with the first.

members.name: init
members.doc:
Creates an iterator that yields every `step` elements.
Panics if step is 0.

members.name: next
members.doc: Returns every nth element.

items.name: TakeIterator
items.signature: public struct TakeIterator[I] where I: Iterator { /* private fields */ }
items.doc: Takes only the first n elements.

members.name: init
members.doc: Creates an iterator that yields at most count elements.

members.name: next
members.doc: Returns the next element if count not reached, or None.

items.name: TakeWhileIterator
items.signature: public struct TakeWhileIterator[I] where I: Iterator { /* private fields */ }
items.doc: Takes elements while predicate is true, then stops.

members.name: init
members.doc: Creates an iterator that yields elements until predicate returns false.

members.name: next
members.doc: Returns the next element if predicate is still true, or None.

items.name: ZipIterator
items.signature: public struct ZipIterator[A, B] where A: Iterator, B: Iterator { /* private fields */ }
items.doc:
Pairs elements from two iterators.
Stops when either iterator is exhausted.

members.name: init
members.doc: Creates an iterator that pairs elements from first and second.

members.name: next
members.doc: Returns the next pair, or None if either iterator is exhausted.

items.name: empty
items.signature: public func empty[T]() -> EmptyIterator[T]
items.doc: Creates an iterator that yields nothing.

items.name: once
items.signature: public func once[T](T) -> OnceIterator[T]
items.doc: Creates an iterator that yields value exactly once.

items.name: repeatN
items.signature: public func repeatN[T](T, Int64) -> RepeatNIterator[T]
items.doc: Creates an iterator that yields value exactly count times.

items.name: repeatValue
items.signature: public func repeatValue[T](T) -> RepeatIterator[T]
items.doc: Creates an iterator that yields value forever.
